personas.persona: Kelly runs the firm the way she runs a search: decide early, tell people the truth, and never let a client be surprised. Her writing is short and unhedged, first names with anyone she has worked with more than once, and she will state the uncomfortable thing in the second sentence rather than bury it. She reserves formality for the documents that bind the firm, where her tone tightens noticeably. When a search is drifting she writes the note herself instead of asking the consultant to.

Sandra owns the search practice and the standards that go with it, and most of the consultants and associates route their work through her. She writes in careful, complete paragraphs, and is the firm's strictest voice on discretion: she will describe a candidate by their seat and their market rather than name them, and she edits other people's drafts to do the same. Her warmth is real but arrives after the substance, usually in a closing line. She is the one who asks what the client actually needs, as opposed to what the position specification says.

Jim came up inside the firm and never lost the habit of picking up the phone before writing anything down, so his emails read like the second half of a conversation you did not hear the start of. He signs internal notes as Jim, though his name appears in full on anything a client or candidate sees, and he uses first names with the clients he has known longest. He is a relationship consultant more than a process one, prone to a digression about someone's old boss, and he is the one who talks a nervous finalist back to the table. Colleagues rely on him for the read on whether a client will actually hire.

Jeffrey came up through research and it still shows: he wants the market mapped before he will opine on a slate, and his position specifications are the ones other consultants quietly copy the structure from. He writes long and precisely, sometimes past the point the reader needed, and he rereads before sending. He has a particular gift for the difficult note, the one that goes out when a finalist withdraws or a client's expectation has to be reset, because he says the thing plainly and then says what happens next. His humor is dry and rare enough that people notice it.

John was a quiet, methodical presence on the research bench, more comfortable building a sourcing list than arguing about it in a room. His written work was clean and heavily organized, with the reasoning laid out so a consultant could pick it up cold. He asked few questions but the ones he asked usually exposed something the consultant had assumed.

James keeps the firm's operations running and treats scheduling as the load-bearing work it actually is in a correspondence business. He always signs with his full name and expects others to do the same, because a second Weiss on the roster makes a first-name reply genuinely ambiguous. His notes are brisk, faintly dry, and never longer than they need to be, and he is the person who notices that an engagement has gone quiet without a client update.

David is fast on the research bench and eager to be useful, the associate who returns a market map sooner than anyone expected and then wants to talk through it. He writes conversationally, sometimes more casually than the consultants would in front of a client, and he is still learning where the firm's discretion line sits. He is careful to distinguish himself from the other Weiss in writing, usually by leading with his first name and letting the context do the rest.

Jason works the phone harder than the keyboard and measures his week in conversations rather than lists. He is competitive about sourcing, mildly proud of finding the candidate nobody else surfaced, and his written summaries are terse because the interesting part already happened out loud. Consultants who want detail from him have to ask a second time.

Nicole handles the research where titles carry real weight and a sloppy description of someone's scope would be noticed immediately, and she is fastidious about getting those right. Her drafts are measured and slightly formal, and she checks a term rather than guess at it. She is unusually good at reading whether a candidate is genuinely interested or being polite, and she says so in her notes without dressing it up.

Larry is blunt in a way that reads as refreshing internally and gets softened before it reaches a client. His research notes are short bullets and a verdict, and he would rather flag a weak slate early than pad it out. He respects the discretion rules but treats the surrounding ceremony as overhead.

Mary writes the cleanest prose on the research bench and is often handed the position specification when the client is particular about how the firm sounds. She asks a lot of clarifying questions up front, which the consultants have learned saves a revision later. Her tone is warm and unhurried, and she has an instinct for describing a candidate's scope vividly without ever putting a name on paper.

Heather is the newest voice on the bench and writes like someone who has read the firm's back catalog closely, borrowing structure from whoever last wrote the document she is imitating. She hedges more than the consultants do and asks permission for things she could simply decide. What she already has is a good ear for the discretion norm: she describes candidates by their seat instinctively, without having to be corrected.
